title: A New Start

jokes.text: Looks like we got ourselves an Anus Tart.
jokes.setup: A surveillance-style TV crew is watching a car approach, treating it like a predator sting operation with serious procedural language.
jokes.punchline: The target is identified by license plate: 'ANUS TART' — Tobias' vanity plate, which the crew reads completely deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly constructed cold open. The tonal whiplash — serious TV-crew surveillance language landing on 'ANUS TART' — is exactly the kind of joke AD excels at. It's character-specific (only Tobias would have this plate), structurally tight, and seeds a running gag that pays off across the entire episode. The deadpan delivery makes it land harder.

jokes.text: What Sudden Valley does to these cell phones!
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to call his daughter; the call drops.
jokes.punchline: He blames Sudden Valley — the narrator clarifies the house has actually been rented to a TV crew, so the dropped call is the least of his problems. Tobias is oblivious to the real situation.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD layering — Tobias confidently misattributes a problem while the narrator and audience know the real reason. His obliviousness is the joke, and it's well-integrated with the ongoing setup.

jokes.text: Yes, Tobias was finally about to get a starring role on a hit TV show. / It's John Beard's To Entrap a Local Predator Supercreeps.
jokes.setup: The narrator says Tobias is 'finally about to get a starring role on a hit TV show,' making it sound like his acting dreams are coming true.
jokes.punchline: The show is revealed to be a predator-sting reality TV program — Tobias is the predator, not the star.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural jokes. The narrator weaponizes his own framing: 'starring role on a hit TV show' is technically true but catastrophically misleading. The punchline — the show name — is absurd and perfect. High craft because it uses the narrator's voice (a show-level tool) against the character's delusion.

jokes.text: It's Tobias' Arrested Development.
jokes.setup: The episode title card format mirrors the show's own opening title sequence; 'Arrested Development' is both the show's name and describes Tobias' psychological/emotional stagnation.
jokes.punchline: 'Arrested Development' means both the show and Tobias being emotionally/professionally stunted. The show uses its own title against its character.
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode. Three meanings collapse into two words: (1) the show's title, (2) Tobias being psychologically/professionally stunted, and (3) the Season 4 format of isolating characters into their own episodes. It's meta without being gratuitous, economically flawless, and character-specific in a way no other show could replicate. This is what AD does better than almost any other sitcom.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. Everyone thinks I'm gay? / Well, I mean, it's kind of a running joke in the family.
jokes.setup: Lindsay tells Tobias his marriage seems like a sham because people think he's gay.
jokes.punchline: Tobias, who has spent years making inadvertent double-entendres, is genuinely shocked. Lindsay confirms it's a 'running joke in the family' — which is also a meta-acknowledgment that it's a running joke on the show.
jokes.explanation: This works on two levels simultaneously: as character comedy (Tobias is the last to know) and as meta-commentary (the show acknowledging its own most famous running gag). The phrase 'running joke in the family' is doing double duty. High character integration — this only works because of years of Tobias double-entendres.

jokes.text: ♪ It's just a fallacy! ♪ / 'Misleading?'
jokes.setup: Tobias insists the way he talks doesn't sound gay, demanding to know when he said anything 'remotely misleading.'
jokes.punchline: He immediately proves the opposite by singing a musical theater-style exclamation — 'It's just a fallacy!' — which is itself misleading in every possible way.
jokes.explanation: Supremely elegant self-own. The joke IS the proof of the thesis being denied. Tobias bursting into a musical one-liner to rebut accusations of sounding gay is perfectly constructed irony. It's instant, economic, and couldn't be anyone else. The single word 'Misleading?' from Lindsay after is a perfect reaction beat that extends it without wasting a word.

jokes.text: Well, you look like I feel. / Gay? / No, I... No.
jokes.setup: Tobias sits with Lindsay's family feeling lost and misunderstood. An older family member offers commiseration.
jokes.punchline: The old man says 'you look like I feel,' and Tobias immediately jumps to 'Gay?' — revealing that 'gay' is now so firmly in his mind that he interprets any ambiguous statement as referring to it.
jokes.explanation: Quick, well-timed callback. Tobias has been primed by the previous scene, and his reflexive 'Gay?' is both character-consistent and escalates the running joke without repeating it exactly. The deflated 'No, I... No.' from George Sr. is perfect economy.

jokes.text: They're still reading it, but... Well, I guess I can leave them some Love. So he just took Eat and Pray.
jokes.setup: Tobias finds a copy of 'Eat, Pray, Love' and takes it for inspiration.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals he only took 'Eat' and 'Pray' — leaving the owners 'some Love' — literalizing the book title as three physical objects and making the theft both polite and absurd.
jokes.explanation: Elegant wordplay that treats the book title as a divisible object. The grammar of 'leave them some Love' is perfect — it's simultaneously generous and absurd, and requires the audience to parse it on two levels simultaneously. AD wordplay at its best.

jokes.text: Eat was easy, although it provided no cosmic answers for him.
jokes.setup: Following the book conceit, the narrator recounts Tobias following the 'Eat' section.
jokes.punchline: The deadpan acknowledgment that eating provided no cosmic answers is both obvious and perfectly stated — the absurdity is in treating food consumption as a legitimate spiritual quest.
jokes.explanation: Efficient extension of the book-title joke. The narrator's dry delivery of an obvious truth ('eating didn't give him enlightenment') is funny precisely because it's stated as if it needed to be confirmed.

jokes.text: And these romantic gestures are possibly why people think I'm a homosexual.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay accidentally grab the same suitcase; Tobias marvels that matching luggage 'isn't romantic anymore.'
jokes.punchline: He then self-diagnoses that these 'romantic gestures' might be why people think he's gay — completely missing that it's not the suitcase sentiment but his entire manner of being.
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy. Tobias' obliviousness is well-calibrated — he's genuinely trying to self-reflect but picking the most absurd specific thing to blame. The mismatch between his diagnosis and the reality is the joke.

jokes.text: A new start. Filled with compassion and love. / I like that. / Well, I've already got the license plate, so go cry in your pie!
jokes.setup: Tobias declares 'a new start filled with compassion and love'; Lindsay seems genuinely moved.
jokes.punchline: He immediately undercuts it by revealing the license plate already exists and dismissing her with 'go cry in your pie' — pure aggression dressed up as inspiration.
jokes.explanation: The 'go cry in your pie' non-sequitur is both aggressive and absurd — it fits Tobias' theatrical bluster. The reveal that the plate already exists anchors the cold-open joke retroactively. The whiplash from 'compassion and love' to 'go cry in your pie' is sharp.

jokes.text: I guess this is why you never see them driving convertibles. / Stay in your lane, anus tart! / Anus tart!
jokes.setup: Tobias has assembled an outfit he thinks is traditional Indian garb to look respectful; he's driving a convertible.
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes convertibles don't work well with Indian turbans/garb (the wind presumably destroys them), and local Indian drivers yell 'anus tart' — reading the plate rather than insulting him.
jokes.explanation: Effective layering — the visual gag of the turban in a convertible is funny, but what elevates it is the irony that the Indian drivers calling him 'anus tart' appears to be an insult but is actually just them reading his plate. Both meanings coexist perfectly.

jokes.text: Well, there we go. India clean.
jokes.setup: Tobias is attempting to adopt Indian customs and dress; he's clearly making a mess of it.
jokes.punchline: 'India clean' as a self-satisfied descriptor implies an entirely different standard of cleanliness — said with complete confidence while presumably looking chaotic.
jokes.explanation: Compact and punchy. 'India clean' does a lot of work in two words, suggesting cultural condescension dressed up as enthusiasm. Slightly cheap in its premise (the 'different standards' implication is a lazy cultural joke) but saved by Tobias' oblivious confidence.

jokes.text: I look like one of those hot guys from Spartacus.
jokes.setup: Tobias is struggling to tie his improvised sarong at Bed, Bath & Beyond with help from Pradeep.
jokes.punchline: His self-assessment is that he looks like a 'hot guy from Spartacus' — revealing his self-image is wildly disconnected from reality, and also that his reference point for attractive men is male actors in revealing costumes.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly character-specific. Only Tobias would (a) have a confident delusion about his appearance, and (b) choose male gladiators as his beauty reference point while missing the implication entirely. Economy is excellent — it says everything about him in one sentence.

jokes.text: Canadian? / No, I'm American. / Yeah. I'm Canadian. / Sorry.
jokes.setup: On a long flight, Tobias in his makeshift Indian garb is presumably a spectacle. Another passenger asks if he's Canadian.
jokes.punchline: The Canadian apologizes — implying that Canadian is somehow an embarrassing thing to be mistaken for, OR that the apology is for the question itself. The beat lands on 'Sorry' — the Canadian stereotype completing the joke.
jokes.explanation: Efficient little throwaway. The Canadian stereotype ('Sorry') is not particularly original but is executed with good economy and the confusion of who's apologizing for what gives it a slight absurdist quality.

jokes.text: How could they not have Failure to Launch?
jokes.setup: Tobias is on a long flight trying to take his mind off Lindsay; he scrolls through in-flight entertainment.
jokes.punchline: He wants to watch 'Failure to Launch' — a romantic comedy about a man-child who can't commit to adulthood. The title is a precise description of Tobias himself.
jokes.explanation: Deceptively strong. The joke works purely on irony — Tobias doesn't see the self-description, but the audience does. 'Failure to Launch' as Tobias' desired comfort film is perfect character integration. Rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Tobias experienced India from the inside of a hospital room... once outside the airport and once in the elevator when his sheet got caught and he was pulled off his gurney. Literally. Two places indeed.
jokes.setup: A doctor says Tobias 'broke his skull in two places.' The narrator matter-of-factly explains what the two places were.
jokes.punchline: The two skull-breaking locations are not regions of the skull but physical locations in India — and a third is added immediately after when he hits the Ganesh statue.
jokes.explanation: Clever exploitation of 'in two places' — the phrase is deliberately ambiguous and the narrator resolves it in the most absurd direction. The escalation to a third break (the Ganesh statue) before the joke has even settled is well-paced AD structure.

jokes.text: It's like that new show we just got, Laugh-In. / My name is... Oh! Now I've broken my skull in a third place! On this elephant guy statue!
jokes.setup: The Indian hospital staff is finding Tobias hilarious; a doctor compares him to a comedy show.
jokes.punchline: Tobias, apparently gesturing during speech, breaks his skull on a Ganesh statue — completing the 'third place' escalation while also being humiliated by the very divine sign he was seeking.
jokes.explanation: The Ganesh statue detail is inspired — it's the 'elephant guy' (Tobias never learns the name), and it's both the divine sign he's been seeking AND the thing that injures him further. The slapstick escalation is well-executed and the 'Laugh-In' joke from the doctor gives it an extra absurdist layer.

jokes.text: You should be a comedy actor. / And finally it had. / This is the sign I've been... Looking for.
jokes.setup: Tobias has been seeking a sign from the universe about his true calling. The doctor says 'you should be a comedy actor' as a joke about how clumsy/funny Tobias is.
jokes.punchline: Tobias takes it as the genuine cosmic sign he was seeking — the narrator confirms 'And finally it had' — missing entirely that it was a dismissive joke at his expense.
jokes.explanation: Beautiful irony. Tobias receives his 'sign' — it's genuine encouragement (he IS comedically gifted, just not intentionally) but completely misread in terms of its sincerity. The narrator's complicity makes it land harder. The pause on 'Looking for' is a nice beat.

jokes.text: Although, as we discovered when our MasterCard bill came back, it was the same one she took.
jokes.setup: Tobias says at a family gathering that he and Lindsay are both on journeys of self-discovery.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals they are literally on the same journey — the MasterCard bill shows Lindsay spent on his trip, meaning she followed him or their accounts are jointly funding both adventures.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator undermining — the show cuts from Tobias's speech to the financial reality. Efficient and characteristically sharp, though the joke requires understanding the Lindsay storyline running parallel.

jokes.text: For 2,000 rupees, we'd both go down on Matthew McConaughey.
jokes.setup: Tobias is defending his $36 hospital debt by contextualizing 2,000 rupees.
jokes.punchline: He casually drops that both he and Lindsay would perform oral sex on Matthew McConaughey for $36 — again, oblivious to what this implies about him.
jokes.explanation: Instantly quotable. The specificity of Matthew McConaughey, the casualness of 'we'd both,' and the '$36' translation make this a dense, layered joke. It's a perfect Tobias double-entendre: he thinks he's making a point about currency; the audience hears a confession. High quotability.

jokes.text: At the time of this family meeting, 2,000 rupees was $36. / I wouldn't. / Of course you wouldn't, Michael. You never accept help from anyone.
jokes.setup: The narrator fact-checks the rupee amount. Michael responds 'I wouldn't' (presumably to the McConaughey offer).
jokes.punchline: Lucille interprets Michael's 'I wouldn't' as being about accepting help — completely recontextualizing his response as a character flaw rather than a moral stance on the sex act.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional comic construction. Three layers operate simultaneously: (1) the narrator's deadpan fact-check, (2) Michael's indignant refusal, (3) Lucille's misinterpretation that transforms Michael's dignity into his most famous character flaw. This is AD at its densest — maximum information per second, each element enriching the others.

jokes.text: Listen, I'm an actor, you're an agent. You do the math. / In other words, you do the math. The money, the negotiating. / You know I'm not that kind of agent, right? I'm a real estate agent. I'm a predator.
jokes.setup: Tobias confidently pitches to what he thinks is a talent agent: 'you do the math.'
jokes.punchline: The 'agent' is a real estate agent who sells giant houses to poor people with predatory loans — and immediately self-identifies as a 'predator,' which also ties into the show's To Entrap a Predator framing.
jokes.explanation: The real estate agent doubling as a 'predator' in the predatory loans sense connects neatly to the show's frame. Tobias explaining his own metaphor ('in other words, you do the math') is also a good character beat — he doesn't trust anyone to keep up with him.

jokes.text: All you need to do is tell people what a terrific actor I am, 'cause I can't do it believably. / Sounds like it shouldn't be too much work.
jokes.setup: Tobias explains that his agent just needs to tell people he's a great actor — because he can't do it convincingly himself.
jokes.punchline: The agent's response — 'shouldn't be too much work' — is a perfect deadpan insult: it agrees with Tobias' framing while implying nobody will believe it regardless.
jokes.explanation: Economically brilliant. Tobias' self-deprecation is unintentional — he thinks he's being practical. The agent's deadpan reading cuts two ways: either because convincing one person is easy, or because no one will need to be convinced because it's not true. Both readings work simultaneously.

jokes.text: Okay, here's the number of my current agent Mike Matthews. He's at Allstate. Fire him.
jokes.setup: Tobias has just engaged a real estate agent to be his talent agent.
jokes.punchline: He then reveals his previous 'agent' was at Allstate — an insurance company — meaning he's been sending his acting materials to an insurance agent. He's had three wrong kinds of agents.
jokes.explanation: Efficient absurdist detail. The escalation from 'real estate agent' to 'Allstate agent' as the previous representation is a quick escalation that reveals the depth of Tobias' cluelessness without belaboring it.

jokes.text: Hi. I'm after pending. / Taft-Hartley pending.
jokes.setup: Tobias sneaks onto a studio lot to hand out headshots. He's wearing a badge.
jokes.punchline: His badge reads 'Taft-Hartley pending' — a union status for actors who aren't yet full SAG members — but he's wearing it as if it's a name badge, introducing himself as 'after pending.'
jokes.explanation: Requires industry knowledge to fully land, which is very AD — it rewards viewers who know what Taft-Hartley means. His misreading of the badge as a name is both a visual gag and a character beat about his desperate desire to belong to the industry.

jokes.text: Including duck l'orange! / Lindsay! Lindsay, get the scooper! / Throw oranges at it. / Hot orange! Hot orange! / It's throwing its voice! Lindsay!
jokes.setup: The Funkes attempt a home-cooked Thanksgiving with duck l'orange.
jokes.punchline: The duck is apparently alive (or fighting back), the oranges are used as weapons, and Tobias claims the duck is 'throwing its voice' — escalating chaos narrated through increasingly panicked outbursts.
jokes.explanation: 'It's throwing its voice!' is the peak of this sequence — an absurdist escalation that only makes sense if the duck is genuinely supernatural, which the panic in Tobias' voice sells completely. The visual gag of using oranges as weapons against a duck is strong physical comedy.

jokes.text: And so they went to the Fountain Valley China Garden instead.
jokes.setup: The Thanksgiving duck fight has just concluded in disaster.
jokes.punchline: The narrator delivers the aftermath in the most mundane possible way — Chinese food on Thanksgiving, reported as if it's a perfectly natural outcome.
jokes.explanation: Good economy. The narrator's flat delivery of the Thanksgiving pivot is the joke. Not especially original but well-executed as a button to the duck sequence.

jokes.text: The suicide of his ruined and despondent agent certainly could [deter Tobias].
jokes.setup: The narrator lists obstacles that failed to stop Tobias: housing collapse, no auditions, Writers' Strike.
jokes.punchline: The agent's suicide is listed as the next logical step in the escalation, delivered with the same flat tone as 'lack of auditions' — treating a death as equivalent to a career inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy that works because of the deadpan escalation structure. The Writers' Strike → suicide escalation is jarring in exactly the right way. The real punchline is in the next joke (30), where Tobias responds.

jokes.text: I believe he died as a warning, in a way, his message perhaps to live within our means and be realistic about our lives. / And yet it didn't. / Well, then I shall redouble my efforts so that he died in vain. / Meaning he will have led a pointless life, is what I mean.
jokes.setup: Tobias interprets his agent's suicide as a cautionary message about realistic expectations.
jokes.punchline: He then decides to honor the agent by ignoring his message — redoubling his efforts so the agent 'died in vain.' Then immediately clarifies 'meaning he will have led a pointless life,' as if that clarification helps.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Tobias correctly interprets the message, then decides to deliberately ignore it as a tribute — which is a perfect inversion of normal grief logic. The kicker ('meaning he will have led a pointless life') is a spectacular own-goal: Tobias trying to clarify but only making himself sound worse. High quotability, strong dark comedy.

jokes.text: Meaning he will have led a pointless life, is what I mean. Yes, carry on. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Tobias has said he'll redouble his efforts 'so that he died in vain.'
jokes.punchline: He then clarifies what 'died in vain' means — that the agent will have led a pointless life — apparently needing to hear himself spell it out, then politely dismissing whoever he's talking to.
jokes.explanation: The follow-up lands its own laugh by having Tobias define his own cruel statement and then politely dismiss his audience. The 'Yes, carry on. Thank you.' is oddly formal and perfect.

jokes.text: What did you say the name of this acting class was again? / Method One Clinic. / Okay, I'm gonna go get coffee. / Garden Grove Method One Clinic.
jokes.setup: Tobias brings Lindsay to an acting class to jump-start their relationship and career.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay asks the name, Tobias says 'Method One Clinic.' She decides to go get coffee. Then Tobias adds 'Garden Grove Method One Clinic' — the full name makes clear it's a drug rehabilitation clinic, which Lindsay immediately clocked and used as her exit.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed. The joke structure is almost musical: setup (Method One Clinic) → Lindsay's departure → full name reveal. The audience realizes exactly when Lindsay did — and the reveal that she bolted the moment she understood is both a character moment and a well-constructed punchline. The 'going to get coffee' beat is the pivot that makes it work.

jokes.text: And I made it in that movie, the Fantastic Four, but it wasn't fantastic for me. / I got carried away. / And I'm like, 'This is up, and I've got to get my together or I'm gonna be too.'
jokes.setup: DeBrie performs what appears to be a personal monologue at the acting/methadone clinic.
jokes.punchline: Her story is clearly real, not rehearsed, and she's leaving out key words (presumably drug-related) that the context fills in: 'this is [messed] up, I've got to get my [life] together or I'm gonna be [too far gone].' The gaps are funnier than words would be.
jokes.explanation: The blanked-out words are a clever device — funnier than explicit language because they force the audience to fill in the gaps, and the blanks themselves signal that she's performing to an audience she expects to censor. Also sets up the show-within-the-show of the methadone clinic mistaken for an acting class.

jokes.text: Okay, notes. First of all, it seemed you were slurring a bit there, and I just think you'd have so much more fun punching those words. / 'This is up, sir.' / I'm sorry. I'm not familiar with the piece, so...
jokes.setup: DeBrie has just given what is clearly a real personal testimony at a methadone clinic. Tobias critiques it as if it's a theatrical performance.
jokes.punchline: He gives acting notes on what was a genuine confession, suggesting she 'punch' the drug references for more dramatic effect, and admits he's 'not familiar with the piece' — treating her life story as a play he hasn't read.
jokes.explanation: Peak Tobias obliviousness. 'I'm not familiar with the piece' applied to someone's actual life is a devastating character joke. The cringe factor is high because he's being actively harmful while genuinely trying to be helpful. 'Punching' the drug words — for theatrical effect — shows exactly how deeply he has misread the room.

jokes.text: Twenty years earlier, DeBrie Bardeaux had played Sue Storm in the low-budget version of the Fantastic Four, produced by Imagine Entertainment, when, during Imagine's Christmas party, a drunk lawyer reminded them they'd lose the rights if they didn't make the film in the next six days. After a two-hour search, they found their entire cast.
jokes.setup: Tobias recognizes DeBrie from the Fantastic Four movie.
jokes.punchline: The backstory reveals the movie was made in desperation after a drunk lawyer reminder, the cast was found in two hours (implying they were literally picked off the street), and everything about it was a disaster.
jokes.explanation: This is a parody of the actual 1994 Roger Corman Fantastic Four film — which really was made purely to hold the rights. The accuracy elevates it from generic mockery to specific, knowledgeable satire. The detail of a 'two-hour search' for the entire cast is the best specific.

jokes.text: They also proved useful six days later at the wrap party.
jokes.setup: The narrator describes finding the cast in two hours for the film shoot.
jokes.punchline: 'They also proved useful six days later at the wrap party' — implying the cast (presumably homeless people or addicts found quickly) were used again for the party, in an unspecified capacity that sounds exploitative.
jokes.explanation: Tremendous economy. The implication is left entirely to the audience — 'proved useful' doing what, exactly? The dark ambiguity is the joke. One of the more purely economical lines in the episode.

jokes.text: I almost never get recognized as the Invisible Girl. / I can't believe you saw me.
jokes.setup: DeBrie is touched that Tobias recognized her from her role as Sue Storm / the Invisible Girl.
jokes.punchline: 'I can't believe you saw me' — her role was literally the Invisible Girl, making 'saw me' a pun. Also true in reality: she's invisible because no one cares about this film.
jokes.explanation: Perfect wordplay. 'I can't believe you saw me' operates on two levels — the Invisible Girl power, and the reality of being an unseen actress. The beauty is that DeBrie probably intends both meanings without realizing it. Four words, two complete jokes. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: Well, I thought it was 'fantastic.' / Actually, most of the performance that had so captivated Tobias... was performed by a fishing line.
jokes.setup: Tobias tells DeBrie he thought her performance was 'fantastic' (double meaning with Fantastic Four).
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals most of her performance was physically achieved by a fishing line — meaning Tobias was captivated by the fishing line's work.
jokes.explanation: The fishing line reveal is perfectly placed — it retroactively demolishes both Tobias' admiration and DeBrie's pride simultaneously. The 'fantastic' pun landing just before the reveal is well-timed. This is the kind of comedy that rewards the instant replay.

jokes.text: Hey, maybe she came into this Light Bulbs Unlimited. / She was here a minute ago. / This is the gayest Starbucks ever! / Did his fishing line break, too?
jokes.setup: DeBrie has turned invisible (performed by a fishing line, now broken); Tobias is looking for her.
jokes.punchline: They're in a Light Bulbs Unlimited store, which Tobias calls 'the gayest Starbucks ever' — a category error that reveals his confusion about the store AND his tendency to label everything. 'Did his fishing line break, too?' from DeBrie connects back to the narrator's reveal.
jokes.explanation: 'The gayest Starbucks ever' is an efficient Tobias-ism — his brain immediately categorizes unfamiliar spaces in familiar (often gay-coded) terms. The callback to the fishing line is clever. The visual of them in a light bulbs store is a nice absurdist staging.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers used to give us grape juice. But then he'd add $5 to our credit card.
jokes.setup: DeBrie mentions Carl Weathers (a recurring AD character known for extreme cheapness) in the context of the acting class.
jokes.punchline: Carl Weathers would give them grape juice but charge $5 for it — a callback to his established character trait of profiting from every generosity.
jokes.explanation: Fan-reward callback that works even without prior knowledge (it's still a funny image) but lands harder for viewers who know Carl Weathers as the cheapest man in Hollywood. The grape juice specificity is a nice detail.

jokes.text: Well, here is to our acting addiction. Yep. May we never be cured.
jokes.setup: Tobias and DeBrie toast at what Tobias thinks is an acting class but is a methadone clinic.
jokes.punchline: 'May we never be cured' is a toast about acting passion — but DeBrie is literally in a clinic to be cured of addiction. The double meaning is dark and precise.
jokes.explanation: Exquisitely layered. The toast works as sincere artistic camaraderie and as a dark irony about DeBrie's addiction. 'May we never be cured' is memorable, quotable, and devastating if you consider DeBrie's trajectory. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: DeBrie, are you crying? Oh, I'm crying. Oh, it's a nosebleed.
jokes.setup: The toast has been emotional; Tobias asks if DeBrie is crying.
jokes.punchline: It's a nosebleed — a drug use symptom — delivered with the same emotional flatness as 'Oh, I'm crying.' The casual pivot from tears to nosebleed implies the nosebleed is a routine occurrence for her.
jokes.explanation: Short, dark, well-calibrated. The pivot from emotional crying to clinical nosebleed is a good tonal whiplash. The humor comes from DeBrie's casualness about her own symptoms — she's been here before.

jokes.text: And after a not-very-cute meet-cute, Tobias continued to find DeBrie's behavior delightful, even when the methadone made her think she was a piece of toast.
jokes.setup: The narrator describes the early stages of Tobias and DeBrie's relationship.
jokes.punchline: 'Even when the methadone made her think she was a piece of toast' — a surreal drug side effect stated with complete narrative normalcy.
jokes.explanation: 'Think she was a piece of toast' is the kind of absurdist specificity that distinguishes great comedy writing. The phrase 'not-very-cute meet-cute' is also precise self-awareness from the narrator about the genre conventions being subverted. High quotability.

jokes.text: Now I can see a pretty face under all that butter substitute.
jokes.setup: Following the toast delusion beat, Tobias wipes something off DeBrie's face.
jokes.punchline: 'Butter substitute' — not butter, but the specific cruelty of hospital/institutional butter substitute coating her face — treats the toast delusion as literal while being precise about the brand of indignity.
jokes.explanation: 'Butter substitute' is funnier than 'butter' — the specificity of the cheap institutional spread adds a layer of sad absurdism. Tobias treating it tenderly makes it simultaneously sweet and dark.

jokes.text: She had been in a series of soft-core porn movies about women leading straight men into gay sex called StraightBait.
jokes.setup: DeBrie says she's done things she's not proud of. Tobias guesses 'Episodics? Been there.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals she was in soft-core pornography films specifically designed to introduce straight men to gay sex — the title 'StraightBait' is exact, and the concept is absurd enough to land on its own.
jokes.explanation: The concept of 'StraightBait' is original and well-named. It also sets up multiple future callbacks throughout the episode. The narrator's deadpan delivery of the premise is pitch-perfect AD style.

jokes.text: If you want to have sex with me, then you've got to have sex with him first. / And if you don't want to have sex with her, you must be gay. / I'll show you how not gay I am.
jokes.setup: We see/hear the premise of the StraightBait film series.
jokes.punchline: The 'logic' of the film is a trap: you either have gay sex (to prove you're straight) or refuse (which proves you're gay). It's a sealed absurdist loop. And the man's response — 'I'll show you how not gay I am' — is the trigger for exactly what they wanted.
jokes.explanation: The logic trap of StraightBait is elegantly constructed — both choices lead to the same outcome. It's a satirical premise about homophobia (the men are so terrified of being called gay they'll do anything), and the compressed three-line structure delivers it efficiently. The absurdist loop is its own complete joke.

jokes.text: She did six of these movies, and after lunch, did three more.
jokes.setup: The narrator is describing DeBrie's filmography.
jokes.punchline: 'After lunch' — the implication being that she filmed nine soft-core films in a single day, with a lunch break. The casual scheduling of it is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Three words — 'and after lunch' — do enormous work. The implication of nine films in one day, the normalcy of 'after lunch,' the hint at DeBrie's rock-bottom career period — all compressed into a single throwaway phrase. Textbook economy.

jokes.text: Marky Bark, sometimes he doesn't know when I'm in the same room. / And sometimes it feels like it's not the face blindness, you know?
jokes.setup: DeBrie is opening up about feeling unseen by her partner, Marky Bark.
jokes.punchline: She acknowledges that Marky Bark has a medical condition (face blindness/prosopagnosia) that prevents him from recognizing faces — but then expresses that sometimes she thinks he's ignoring her intentionally, despite the diagnosis.
jokes.explanation: Marky Bark's face blindness is introduced here as a character detail that will pay off in other episodes. The joke works on its own: the pathos of someone wondering if their partner's medical condition is just an excuse to ignore them is both funny and genuinely sad. AD at its best uses comedy and character pain simultaneously.

jokes.text: I've yet to make more money as an actor than I ever did as a doctor. And that's with CountryWide behind me.
jokes.setup: Tobias is lamenting his acting career's financial returns.
jokes.punchline: 'And that's with CountryWide behind me' — Countrywide was the predatory mortgage lender that collapsed spectacularly in the 2008 financial crisis, and here it's also the name of his real estate agent (who does predatory loans). Tobias references having a notoriously failed institution 'behind him' as a badge of support.
jokes.explanation: The Countrywide reference ties together the real estate agent subplot with the larger economic backdrop of the show's timeline. It requires the audience to connect the dots — a rewarding joke for attentive viewers.

jokes.text: You're a doctor, and you're out of work. / Well... / You can write scripts? / Well, I like to think so. / You should never give up a dream if you can write scripts.
jokes.setup: DeBrie asks if Tobias, as a doctor, can 'write scripts' — meaning prescriptions.
jokes.punchline: Tobias hears 'scripts' as screenplays/acting scripts, says 'I like to think so' with artistic pride. DeBrie's advice — 'never give up a dream if you can write scripts' — is advice about maintaining a drug supply, not artistic fulfillment.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect double meaning. 'Writing scripts' means prescriptions to DeBrie and screenplays to Tobias; both are completely in character. The advice 'never give up a dream if you can write scripts' is sincere acting encouragement and a drug addict's practical advice simultaneously. This is the kind of layered wordplay that makes AD exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, I like to think so. You should never give up a dream if you can write scripts. You seem like you'd be a good doctor-actor.
jokes.setup: DeBrie asks if Tobias can write scripts (meaning prescriptions). He answers about screenwriting.
jokes.punchline: DeBrie, pivoting to manipulation, tells him 'you should never give up a dream if you can write scripts' — framing prescription access as a reason to maintain his doctor credentials. 'Doctor-actor' is the perfect absurd career label.
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the scripts double meaning. 'Doctor-actor' as a career label is delightfully absurd and very Arrested Development.

jokes.text: You remind me of Billy Crystal... / It would have broken Tobias' heart if he'd known she was about to say 'Billy Crystal Meth,' a funny drug dealer.
jokes.setup: DeBrie begins a compliment: 'You remind me of Billy Crystal...' Tobias is visibly flattered.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals she was going to say 'Billy Crystal Meth' — a drug dealer, not the actor. The name is both a drug reference and a celebrity name pun.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. 'Billy Crystal Meth' is a perfect pun — it's both a celebrity name and a drug reference, and it works as a character name for a drug dealer. The structure (false setup → narrator intervention → reveal) is flawless. The repeated version at 15:24 with a second attempt is well-calibrated escalation. This is comparable to the best AD wordplay across all seasons.

jokes.text: You remind me of Billy Crystal... / Once again, she was going to say, 'Billy Crystal Meth.'
jokes.setup: DeBrie tries the compliment again.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'once again' makes this a confirmed running gag — DeBrie never completes the sentence, and the narrator has become the audience's accomplice in knowing what's coming.
jokes.explanation: The repetition is well-judged — 'once again' from the narrator is funny because it implies the audience should have expected this. The joke lands differently the second time: less surprise, more delight at the structure of the gag itself.

jokes.text: Did you see my license plate, 'A-N-U-S-T-A-R-T'? / Did you see my StraightBait movie of the same name?
jokes.setup: Tobias and DeBrie are both excited by coincidences connecting them.
jokes.punchline: Both reference the title 'ANUSTART' — Tobias from his license plate, DeBrie from a StraightBait film of the same name. The collision of these two running gags is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: The convergence of two running gags is a master-class AD construction. The license plate and the StraightBait film sharing a name is a coincidence so perfectly absurd it feels inevitable in retrospect. It also functions as the universe confirming their 'meant to be' connection — mirroring the show's cosmic sign motif — while being completely ridiculous.

jokes.text: ♪ Coincidence ♪
jokes.setup: The two ANUS TART coincidences have just landed.
jokes.punchline: A single sung word — 'Coincidence' — from what appears to be the score/narrator, delivered as if it's settling the question definitively.
jokes.explanation: One word doing maximum work. The musical delivery of 'Coincidence' as a narrative comment is both meta and perfectly economic. It's the show's narrator winking at the audience about the absurdity of the plot contrivance — and it completely owns the contrivance rather than hiding it.

jokes.text: Can I just improv my way out of a marriage? / Yes, and let's do this. / Blackout.
jokes.setup: Tobias wonders aloud how to leave his marriage for DeBrie.
jokes.punchline: 'Can I improv my way out of a marriage?' is self-aware acting-world language applied to real life. DeBrie's 'Yes, and' is the improv rule (always accept the premise), and 'Blackout' is a scene-ending call — they're literally ending the scene of their old lives.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. Three lines, three complete improv concepts (improv as life-navigation, 'Yes, and' as commitment, Blackout as scene closure) all applied to a real-life decision. The layers: (1) Tobias using acting vocabulary for life choices, (2) DeBrie responding in-kind using the actual improv rule, (3) 'Blackout' as both dramatic irony and literal decision. This rivals the title card joke for best in episode.

jokes.text: If you're wondering what 'too' referred to, it was this... / Let's do this. / Blackout. / And scene, as they say. / Her heart stopped! She's dying!
jokes.setup: Tobias told Lindsay that DeBrie 'stopped his heart too.' The narrator clarifies what 'too' meant.
jokes.punchline: The 'too' referred to DeBrie literally going into cardiac arrest during or after the encounter. The scene cuts to chaos — 'She's a very famous actress!' — treating the celebrity status as the emergency.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's callback to 'too' is a structural flourish — explaining a word that didn't need explanation but reveals a second meaning. The hospital chaos that follows ('She's a very famous actress!') is well-escalated.

jokes.text: Someone say 'butter' out here? / Oh, my God, that's a butter face. / How much butter did the patient have? / I don't know. A Star Trek chess set's worth.
jokes.setup: The hospital chaos around DeBrie's cardiac arrest. Tobias has been yelling 'She had too much butter!' (from the toast delusion).
jokes.punchline: 'Butter face' is slang for an unattractive face, recontextualized as a medical diagnosis. 'A Star Trek chess set's worth' is a ludicrously specific and absurd unit of measurement for butter consumption.
jokes.explanation: 'A Star Trek chess set's worth' is the standout specific. The 'butter face' pun works as an accidental insult Tobias doesn't register. The density of jokes in this section is high — several small hits packed into a short sequence.

jokes.text: Damn it, that Swappigan's puts more people in this hospital... / We got a 61-year-old male in full cardiac arrest coming through. / Be careful of her face! She's an actress!
jokes.setup: The hospital receives DeBrie; simultaneously, another cardiac patient arrives.
jokes.punchline: The Swappigan's restaurant is a running gag (from the Lindsay storyline); the 61-year-old cardiac patient is presumably a Lucille or George Sr. callback. Tobias' priority of protecting DeBrie's face (for her career) amid a cardiac emergency is perfectly in character.
jokes.explanation: Good connective tissue between episode storylines. The convergence of Tobias/DeBrie and the other character's cardiac at the same hospital is a classic AD weave. 'Be careful of her face!' is a perfectly Tobias moment — prioritizing aesthetics over survival.

jokes.text: How much butter did the patient have? I don't know. A Star Trek chess set's worth.
jokes.setup: The ER doctor asks how much butter the patient consumed.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's answer — 'A Star Trek chess set's worth' — is a completely meaningless unit of measurement for butter. It implies a specific quantity (chess set = 32 pieces) but no one knows what that means in butter terms.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully absurdist non-unit of measurement. Tobias tries to answer a medical question with 'a Star Trek chess set's worth of butter' — the specificity (Star Trek, not just chess) makes it funnier. The doctor presumably has no idea what this means either.

jokes.text: We're both actors!
jokes.setup: Tobias has heard the hospital is frequented by show business figures seeking plastic surgery. He's trying to get in.
jokes.punchline: His credential for emergency access to a cardiac patient: 'We're both actors!' — as if actor status grants hospital privileges.
jokes.explanation: Compact character joke. The delusion that being an actor is a medical credential is very Tobias. Good economy in the delivery — one line that reveals everything about his priorities.

jokes.text: I'm afraid that you have tested positive for chlamydia, syphilis, gonorrhea, hepatitis, pancreatitis, trichomonitis, non-gonococcal urethritis. All the itises, really, and there's also a host of osises. / Now, what this means in non-medical terms is that you have landed a real party girl.
jokes.setup: The doctor reads DeBrie's test results — an escalating list of infections.
jokes.punchline: 'All the itises, really, and there's also a host of osises' — the doctor abandons precision for category. Then translates it all into: 'you have landed a real party girl.'
jokes.explanation: 'All the itises, really' is a masterpiece of comedic exhaustion — the doctor giving up on listing them and switching to categories. The translation into 'real party girl' is a dark euphemism that does two things: humanizes DeBrie's condition and reveals the doctor's inappropriate bedside manner. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: You didn't tell me you had a drug problem. / We met at a methadone clinic. / Are you high? We met at an acting class. / The Method One... / There it is. I'm gonna let you guys hash this out.
jokes.setup: The revelation that the 'acting class' was actually a methadone clinic.
jokes.punchline: The doctor sees Tobias genuinely arguing that a methadone clinic is an acting class, says 'There it is,' and immediately excuses himself — the most professional response to Tobias-level delusion.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the acting class/methadone clinic misdirection is well-earned. The doctor's 'There it is. I'm gonna let you guys hash this out.' is the perfect reaction to a man arguing earnestly that his girlfriend isn't a drug addict because they met at an acting class. The doctor's exit is a great reaction beat.

jokes.text: DeBrie, look, some people struggle with addiction. Other people are incapable of ever being nude. Everybody has something.
jokes.setup: Tobias is being supportive of DeBrie's drug problem.
jokes.punchline: He equates drug addiction with being incapable of nudity — his own most famous and well-documented condition, presented as equivalent to heroin addiction. The compassion is real; the comparison is delusional.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's peak jokes. 'Other people are incapable of ever being nude' is a perfect encapsulation of Tobias — he presents his never-nude condition as a parallel to drug addiction, with complete sincerity. 'Everybody has something' as a conclusion is genuinely moving AND perfectly absurd. Cannot be delivered by anyone other than Tobias. Near-perfect character integration.

jokes.text: When I first saw StraightBait, there were two men making love, but I couldn't take my eyes off of you. / You watched StraightBait? / Oh, just two, four and nine.
jokes.setup: Tobias is complimenting DeBrie's acting in StraightBait.
jokes.punchline: His claim that he watched for her acting ('couldn't take my eyes off you') despite two men making love in the frame collapses when he reveals he watched episodes 2, 4, AND 9 — specificity that implies he didn't just stumble on it once.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'just two, four and nine' is the key. 'Just' implies he's being restrained; the specific episode numbers imply he has the full catalogue memorized. The 'just' does all the work — it's Tobias trying to minimize something that cannot be minimized. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: You watched StraightBait? Oh, just two, four and nine.
jokes.setup: DeBrie is surprised Tobias has seen StraightBait.
jokes.punchline: 'Just two, four and nine' — Tobias has seen specific episodes (2, 4, and 9), presented as if it's a reasonable, minimal amount of gay pornography to have consumed.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Just two, four and nine' implies restraint while revealing Tobias has watched nearly a third of the StraightBait catalog. The 'just' is the perfect qualifier — as if three episodes of gay pornography is the responsible, limited amount. The economy is near-perfect. On rewatch, cross-referenced with 'six, then three more after lunch,' you can calculate exactly which volumes he watched.

jokes.text: Come one, come all! Get your photo taken with Sue Storm from the Fantastic Four! / Hey, is that Jessica Alba? / No, this is the original Sue Storm, DeBrie Bardeaux! / Who?
jokes.setup: Tobias is promoting DeBrie as the 'original Sue Storm' on Hollywood Boulevard.
jokes.punchline: People only know Jessica Alba's version; DeBrie is completely unrecognized. 'Who?' is the final indignity.
jokes.explanation: Solid but predictable. The callback to DeBrie's invisibility is well-integrated, and 'Who?' is a satisfying button. Not especially original — celebrity obscurity jokes have a low ceiling — but functionally effective.

jokes.text: I do have a law degree. / I know, I know, and I'm a doctor. But no, let's not settle.
jokes.setup: They've failed at the photo scheme and are considering getting regular jobs.
jokes.punchline: DeBrie has a law degree; Tobias is a doctor. Both are wildly over-qualified for their actual situation. Tobias' response — 'let's not settle' — frames abandoning professional careers as settling.
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. The absurdity of two credentialed professionals treating their credentials as a last resort, to be avoided at all costs, is well-calibrated irony. 'Let's not settle' is a nice inversion of normal ambition language.

jokes.text: They're the Ambiguously Gay Duo. / I assure you, there's nothing ambiguous about me. I'm Johnny Storm, the Human Flamer.
jokes.setup: A child asks if the blue-costumed Tobias and DeBrie are the Smurfs. A bystander suggests the Ambiguously Gay Duo.
jokes.punchline: Tobias denies ambiguity, then announces he's 'the Human Flamer' — the Fantastic Four character is Human Torch/Johnny Storm, but Tobias calls himself the 'Human Flamer,' a gay slur he uses completely unselfconsciously.
jokes.explanation: The single funniest moment in the episode by impact. Tobias explicitly denying ambiguity about his sexuality while immediately calling himself 'the Human Flamer' — a name he invented — is a perfect union of the show's central character joke. The specificity of 'there's nothing ambiguous about me' as the setup makes the punchline devastating. Cannot be said by any other character. On par with the series' best Tobias moments.

jokes.text: And this is Sue Storm, my sister and fellow fighter-in-law.
jokes.setup: Tobias is introducing DeBrie as Sue Storm.
jokes.punchline: 'Fighter-in-law' — combining 'sister-in-law' and 'fighter,' as if 'in-law' can be applied to superhero team dynamics. It's also slightly wrong in both directions simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: 'Fighter-in-law' is a nice wordplay construction. Not as strong as the 'Human Flamer' beat, but it shows Tobias improvising in character and revealing he doesn't quite know the lore.

jokes.text: Mr. Feinberg? Would it change your mind if I offered you a free Sue Storm armband? / It did not change his mind.
jokes.setup: Feinberg & Feinberg (Marvel's lawyers) have served a cease and desist.
jokes.punchline: Tobias tries to bribe/persuade one of Marvel's lawyers with a free Sue Storm armband — a prop from their own costume venture. The narrator's 'It did not change his mind' delivers the expected outcome as a punchline.
jokes.explanation: Good structure. The offer of a free armband to a Marvel attorney is absurd enough to earn the laugh, and the narrator's flat follow-up ('It did not change his mind') is a perfectly economic button.

jokes.text: And although the hate crime charges were dismissed, they were told they could not continue to pose as the Fantastic Four.
jokes.setup: The legal encounter has concluded.
jokes.punchline: The narrator casually mentions 'hate crime charges' that were dismissed — implying that somewhere between the cease and desist and the resolution, hate crime charges were filed and resolved, all off-screen and unremarked upon.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator trick — inserting a significant detail (hate crime charges!) as a throwaway subordinate clause, as if it barely warrants mention. The contrast between the gravity of 'hate crime charges' and the breezy narrative tone is the joke.

jokes.text: Can they really make us leave Hollywood Boulevard? / Well, apparently in this instance, paper beats rock. / You have a rock? / No, I am your rock now.
jokes.setup: Tobias is dressed as The Thing (a rock creature); they've been served a cease and desist ('paper').
jokes.punchline: 'Paper beats rock' as a legal metaphor works because Tobias IS rock (The Thing costume). DeBrie's literal 'You have a rock?' and Tobias' sincere 'I am your rock now' (romantic declaration) make it operate on three levels simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of compressed comedy. Three meanings in four lines: (1) rock-paper-scissors as legal metaphor, (2) Tobias literally being rock (in costume), (3) 'I am your rock' as romantic promise. The fact that DeBrie parses it literally ('You have a rock?') before Tobias pivots to the romantic meaning is perfect comedy structure. This is among the episode's best.

jokes.text: Ability to Be Invisible Person. / Actually, technically, I'm Rock Monster, and this is Ability to Be Invisible Person.
jokes.setup: Tobias and DeBrie can't use the Fantastic Four names. They've rebranded with generic knockoffs.
jokes.punchline: The off-brand names — 'Rock Monster' and 'Ability to Be Invisible Person' — are so descriptively literal they drain all the appeal from the characters while technically meaning the same thing.
jokes.explanation: 'Ability to Be Invisible Person' is a great piece of absurdist naming — it's so literally accurate that it becomes meaningless. The joke satirizes copyright law (you can describe a thing but not name it) and Tobias' complete lack of marketing instinct simultaneously.

jokes.text: What brings you to at least 500 feet past the entrance of Disneyland?
jokes.setup: Tobias and DeBrie have relocated to a street 'near' Disneyland that 'they don't police very much.'
jokes.punchline: The officer greets them by establishing their precise legal distance from Disneyland — implying either a restraining order distance or a legal boundary — in a perfectly procedural tone.
jokes.explanation: 'At least 500 feet past the entrance of Disneyland' is a beautifully specific legal geography joke. The 500-foot restraining-order distance from a family entertainment venue, stated as a matter-of-fact greeting, implies far more than it says about their legal situation.

jokes.text: They're the Fantastic Four. / Actually, technically, I'm Rock Monster... / We're The Thing and the Invisible Girl. / You're under arrest.
jokes.setup: Tobias caves to the child's description of them as the Fantastic Four — abandoning the legal off-brand names for the actual character names.
jokes.punchline: The moment he admits they're 'The Thing and the Invisible Girl,' the officer arrests them — Tobias' capitulation to a child's recognition is the direct cause of his arrest.
jokes.explanation: Strong structural joke — Tobias' weakness (can't resist being recognized) directly causes his downfall. The timing of the arrest is precise: the exact moment he speaks the trademarked names. Good cause-and-effect comedy.

jokes.text: We're not gonna kill him, we're just gonna handcuff him. You can't shoot us 'cause it's not a felony.
jokes.setup: Tobias and DeBrie are being arrested near Disneyland for impersonating characters.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's legal knowledge — 'you can't shoot us 'cause it's not a felony' — reveals he's familiar enough with use-of-force law to know exactly how much trouble he's in, while also being in a rock monster costume.
jokes.explanation: Solid dark moment — Tobias's legal knowledge is oddly specific and delivered as reassurance to DeBrie. The contrast between rock costume and legal knowledge is funny.

jokes.text: If it happens again, you know, it will be a third strike. / Three-strikes law's up for review in the appellate courts, but we really shouldn't be charging.
jokes.setup: The officers are discussing Tobias' legal situation.
jokes.punchline: One officer warns about three strikes; the other is casually discussing appellate review of the law as if this is a normal work conversation — while simultaneously processing a man in a rock monster suit.
jokes.explanation: Functional background comedy. The officers having a nuanced legal discussion in this context is mildly absurd. Not especially original or character-specific, but works as texture.

jokes.text: We're broke. / But you can't sell your Fantastic Four costume. I mean, that would be like a fisherman selling his fishing costume.
jokes.setup: DeBrie says they're broke and presumably tries to sell the costume.
jokes.punchline: Tobias' metaphor — 'like a fisherman selling his fishing costume' — is wonderfully wrong. Fishermen don't have 'fishing costumes'; the comparison reveals Tobias understands both fishing AND acting as primarily costume-based professions.
jokes.explanation: 'Fishing costume' is a perfect Tobias-ism. The joke is that actors wear costumes; fishermen wear practical gear; but to Tobias, who filters everything through performance, fishermen must also have costumes. It's a small window into his worldview and it's completely character-specific.

jokes.text: No, I was gonna have sex with him. / For money. / What? No. Put that back on.
jokes.setup: DeBrie says she'll do something to solve their financial problem. Tobias assumes she's going to sell the costume.
jokes.punchline: She's offering to sell sex, not the costume. 'What? No. Put that back on.' — Tobias' priority is protecting the costume, not the dignity concern.
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection. The 'Put that back on' is the tell — Tobias' concern is the costume being removed (presumably for the sex work), not the sex work itself. His priority system is revealed in three words.

jokes.text: Hey, I'm not a thing. / Hey, I am a thing.
jokes.setup: DeBrie says 'I'm not a thing' — meaning she's not an object to be sold for sex.
jokes.punchline: Tobias responds 'I am a thing' — meaning he's in the Thing costume, but the juxtaposition with DeBrie's statement makes it sound like he's claiming to be an object.
jokes.explanation: Perfect micro-joke. Four words, two complete meanings in counterpoint. DeBrie asserts personhood; Tobias accidentally undermines his own by claiming to be a Thing. The economy is near-perfect — could not be shorter without losing the joke.

jokes.text: Michael. / No, George Michael. / Somebody, help!
jokes.setup: Tobias bursts into the Bluth penthouse to find it ransacked and calls out for Michael.
jokes.punchline: It's George Michael, not Michael — the Bluth family name confusion that's a recurring gag — delivered just before 'Somebody, help!' which now reads as Tobias dismissing George Michael as insufficient help.
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag callback. The Michael/George Michael confusion is well-established; the comedy here is the speed of dismissal — Tobias barely registers who it is before calling for better help. Not the strongest use of the gag.

jokes.text: And that's when he heard what sounded like two ostriches making love to a pop hit. / But was actually this. / ♪ Why don't you go away, go away / Get away, get away, stay away / You're hopelessly hopeless ♪
jokes.setup: Tobias hears something alarming from another room.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's description — 'two ostriches making love to a pop hit' — is so specific and absurd that whatever the actual sound is will be a punchline by comparison.
jokes.explanation: 'Two ostriches making love to a pop hit' is wonderfully specific as a sound description. The contrast with the actual song ('hopelessly hopeless') is also ironic — the song's lyrics describe both Tobias and the situation. Strong absurdist narrator work.

jokes.text: You know, the doctor said if that bird had gotten through my second layer of makeup, it could've done permanent damage to my skin.
jokes.setup: Tobias has just heroically saved Lucille 2 from an ostrich attack.
jokes.punchline: 'Second layer of makeup' — implying her makeup is thick enough to be a protective barrier against bird attacks. The line is delivered as a medical observation.
jokes.explanation: Efficient character joke for Lucille 2 — the suggestion that her foundation is structurally load-bearing is a good vanity gag. 'Second layer' implies additional layers beneath, which is funnier than 'layer.'

jokes.text: How would you like to come work for me at Austerity? / The rehab center? That's yours? / Well, my brother Argyle runs it day-to-day.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 offers Tobias a job at her rehab center, Austerity.
jokes.punchline: The rehab center is called 'Austerity' — a name that perfectly captures both the austerity measures of rehab AND the punishing economic climate. Also: Tobias has just been described as needing professional help himself.
jokes.explanation: The name 'Austerity' for a rehab center is good wordplay — it works both as an accurate description of rehab and as an economic/political term. Sets up future storylines. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: It would only pay 120 grand a year to start. / Tobias, whose girlfriend had nearly sold her body for money in front of him, knew that everyone at some time had to face reality. / Ummmmmm... / But this wasn't the time. No.
jokes.setup: Tobias is offered $120,000/year at Austerity.
jokes.punchline: The narrator builds to Tobias 'facing reality'... then delivers 'But this wasn't the time. No.' The drawn-out 'Ummmmmm' followed by rejection of a $120K job offer is the peak of his character's self-destructive delusion.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's feint toward character growth — 'knew that everyone at some time had to face reality' — followed by 'But this wasn't the time' is a perfected AD narrative move. The setup implies growth; the immediate reversal confirms stasis. Tobias turning down $120K while his girlfriend nearly sold herself is darkly funny and character-definitive.

jokes.text: You turned it down? Mmm-hmm. / I almost a guy for a hamburger, and you turned down $120,000 a year?
jokes.setup: DeBrie learns Tobias turned down the $120K job.
jokes.punchline: 'I almost a guy for a hamburger' — DeBrie leaves out the verb (presumably 'slept with') but we fill it in. The bathos of 'a hamburger' as the compensation is devastating. She was going to prostitute herself for a hamburger.
jokes.explanation: The missing verb is funnier than any word could be. 'I almost [verb] a guy for a hamburger' forces the audience to supply the word, making them complicit. The contrast between 'a hamburger' and '$120,000 a year' is the perfectly calibrated escalation. One of the episode's most quotable and darkly funny moments.

jokes.text: After all, a doctor in India told me that I have the gift to make people laugh. / Why are you crying? / You're worse than Marky. He couldn't see me, but you can't hear me.
jokes.setup: Tobias cites his 'gift' as justification for the career decision; DeBrie is visibly upset.
jokes.punchline: DeBrie's comparison: Marky Bark couldn't see her (face blindness); Tobias can't hear her (emotional deafness). The parallel is devastating — Tobias is no better than the man she left, just differently oblivious.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured comparison. 'He couldn't see me, but you can't hear me' is both a punchline and genuine emotional content — which is the AD sweet spot. The callback to Marky Bark's face blindness gives it structural weight. The line is quotable and genuinely illuminating about Tobias' flaw.

jokes.text: You're worse than Marky. He couldn't see me, but you can't hear me.
jokes.setup: DeBrie is devastated that Tobias turned down financial security, comparing him unfavorably to her previous partner.
jokes.punchline: Marky Bark's face blindness means he couldn't SEE her; Tobias's obliviousness means he can't HEAR her — she's emotionally invisible to both men in different ways.
jokes.explanation: A structurally elegant comparison — face blindness vs. emotional deafness — that is both a good joke and a genuine character observation. DeBrie's pattern of choosing partners who can't perceive her is both funny and quietly dark. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: Who are you supposed to be? / I'm The Thing from Fantastic Four. / You've been served.
jokes.setup: Tobias, still in the Thing costume, is approached by someone in the street.
jokes.punchline: The person asking about his costume is a process server who has been looking for him. He answers the identity question and is immediately served.
jokes.explanation: Efficient. The process server using the costume question to establish identity before serving papers is a well-constructed trap. Short and sharp.

jokes.text: Who are you supposed to be? I'm The Thing from Fantastic Four. You've been served.
jokes.setup: A process server approaches Tobias, apparently in costume or undercover, and asks who he is.
jokes.punchline: After Tobias identifies himself as The Thing, the server serves him papers. The absurdity of being legally served while in a rock monster costume is peak Tobias.
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline. The word 'served' does double duty (information-served and legally-served). Efficient and funny.

jokes.text: Are you undercover as well? / I'm CIA. Not here for you, though. / Scoping out Imagine Entertainment.
jokes.setup: While waiting in his rock suit, Tobias encounters someone else in what might be a costume.
jokes.punchline: The person is CIA, and they're surveilling Imagine Entertainment — the production company that made the Fantastic Four film, connecting the CIA to the absurd Fantastic Four mythology.
jokes.explanation: A funny absurdist detail that retroactively makes the Imagine Entertainment joke darker and more conspiratorial. The CIA surveilling a film company for the rights dispute is a paranoid escalation of the Fantastic Four mythology.

jokes.text: Daddy needs to get his rocks off.
jokes.setup: Tobias arrives at Maeby's door dressed as The Thing (a rock creature) and explains why he's there.
jokes.punchline: 'Get his rocks off' is a sex phrase, but Tobias literally means he needs help removing the rock costume. He's dressed as a rock. The double meaning is entirely unintentional on his part.
jokes.explanation: One of the purest Tobias double-entendres in the episode. The conditions are perfect: (1) he's literally dressed as a rock, (2) the phrase 'get his rocks off' has an established double meaning, (3) 'Daddy' as his self-reference adds an additional layer of wrongness, (4) he's saying this to his daughter. All four layers coexist without Tobias suspecting any of them. Quintessential Tobias, quintessential AD.

jokes.text: On the next To Entrap a Local Predator... / Predator gets its first rock-solid case.
jokes.setup: The episode tag returns to the To Entrap a Predator framing.
jokes.punchline: 'Rock-solid case' — Tobias in a rock costume, arriving at what looks like a predator sting, gives the show its 'first rock-solid case.' The pun connects to both the legal language and the costume.
jokes.explanation: 'Rock-solid case' connecting to Tobias in a rock costume is a satisfying convergence of the episode's two main threads. The callback to the cold open structure is well-executed.

jokes.text: I'm here to see my little girl. / I wanted to show her Daddy's Thing.
jokes.setup: Tobias is being questioned on camera by John Beard in the predator sting.
jokes.punchline: 'Show her Daddy's Thing' — The Thing costume, which he wants to show his daughter. But in the context of a predator sting interrogation, the double meaning is maximally incriminating.
jokes.explanation: Another tier-one Tobias double-entendre. 'Show her Daddy's Thing' is maximally damning in a predator-sting context, and completely innocent in Tobias' mind (he means the rock costume). The callback to both the Thing costume and the show's predator framing makes this a multi-threaded payoff. High quotability.

jokes.text: How old is she? / I want to say 19? / But I like to think of her as 15.
jokes.setup: John Beard is establishing Maeby's age on camera for the predator sting.
jokes.punchline: 'I want to say 19? But I like to think of her as 15' — Tobias has lost track of his own daughter's age, starts with a guess, then reveals he has a preferred younger age in mind. In context of the sting, this is deeply incriminating; Tobias means he thinks of her as young because she'll 'always be his little girl.'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional cringe-comedy construction. Tobias' 'I want to say 19?' shows he genuinely doesn't know his daughter's age. The 'But I like to think of her as 15' is a complete catastrophe in context — a parental sentiment ('she'll always be my little girl') that reads as the most predatory thing imaginable. The question mark after '19' is doing crucial work.

jokes.text: Well, you're free to go. She's not here. / But this creep won't take 'go' for an answer. / That's all right. I'll stay, I'll wait.
jokes.setup: John Beard lets Tobias go since the 'decoy' isn't there.
jokes.punchline: Tobias, who has genuine fatherly reasons to stay, insists on waiting — which John Beard's voiceover narrates as 'this creep won't take go for an answer.'
jokes.explanation: The narrative gap between John Beard's framing ('this creep') and Tobias' actual innocent intent is the engine of this sequence. His willingness to wait — a fatherly virtue — reads as predatory persistence. Good sustained irony.

jokes.text: Tell him there's a raccoon in the backyard. / Hey, guess what? There's a raccoon out on the patio! / Where is that little guy?
jokes.setup: The predator-catching crew needs to get Tobias outside so they can confront him. They plant a raccoon story.
jokes.punchline: Tobias immediately and enthusiastically goes to find the raccoon — 'Where is that little guy?' — completely defeating the crew's plan by treating the predator-confrontation setup as a wholesome wildlife search.
jokes.explanation: 'Where is that little guy?' — Tobias' genuine delight at the prospect of a backyard raccoon, treating the predator show's ruse as an invitation to wildlife observation. His enthusiasm subverts the confrontation setup and shows his fundamental innocuousness.

jokes.text: Tell him there's a raccoon in the backyard. / Hey, guess what? There's a raccoon out on the patio! / Where is that little guy?
jokes.setup: The crew tries to lure Tobias outside with a false raccoon report.
jokes.punchline: Tobias' response — 'Where is that little guy?' — is enthusiastic and childlike, suggesting he genuinely wants to find the raccoon. He exits for the raccoon, not because they've outwitted him but because he likes raccoons.
jokes.explanation: 'Where is that little guy?' is a genuinely funny character beat — Tobias' enthusiastic hunt for a fictional raccoon reveals his childlike affect. The crew expected reluctance; they get enthusiasm for an entirely different reason.

jokes.text: And this guy turns out to be a real comedian. / I just broke my skull in a fourth place. The patio!
jokes.setup: The skull-breaking motif has been running since India (three places). Tobias falls on the patio.
jokes.punchline: 'I just broke my skull in a fourth place. The patio!' — the escalation to four skull breaks is a perfect callback to the India sequence. John Beard's 'real comedian' comment echoes the Indian doctor's assessment.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding episode-closing callback. The skull-breaking gag has been threaded through the entire episode; landing its fourth iteration at the climax of the predator sting, narrated by John Beard as confirmation of Tobias' comedy gift, ties the India arc and the predator arc together in one line. 'The patio!' as a specific location delivered with outrage is the perfect button.

tmdb_overview: Tobias searches for meaning after splitting with Lindsay, only to find that old habits die hard.
insight: Wordplay-heavy Tobias episode sustains 91.5 score through character-rooted irony across 44 jokes.
